author: @marcfruchtman9473
text:
This is quite the amazing design. I wouldn't worry about the size quite yet, because you are likely going to put this at the center of the wheel, so even if it is slightly bigger, there's plenty of room.
I have three concerns, My first concern is regarding the motion of the lever. I notice that when you move the lever, there is concomitant motion of the shaft. I wonder, then, will that interfere if the shaft is direct mounted to the wheel and tire or can the tire rotate freely.
My second is similar, that there likely needs to be a sprocket type action to allow the bike to spin freely without forcing the pedals to turn.
My third is I can't tell if the motion is smooth, or if the converter makes the motion sort of jerky? (I didn't get a good feel for that in the video). 
Overall tho, this look like it has huge potential.

author: @lostvisitor
text:
Love the excitement and designs.  Sadly I don't think they will be very practical.  There will be lots of loss in all the moving parts and it will break when torque is applied.  (there is just too little material at the points of contact.)

But keep up the great work.

author: @Simon_Rafferty

Why don't you reach out to one of the companies offering Metal Printing - and see if they will sponsor building a real, practical bike CVT?
It would be interesting to measure the transmission efficiency against a regular hub reduction with a similar ratio range.
Once again, a good video.

author: @Hukkinen
text:
I feel it intriguing that you, an expert in your field, still haven't exhausted the space of all possible gear mechaniams. That you still have solutions to uncover and design.

This is not my field. I would have assumed all the gear mechanisms have already consolidated 🤔😮 but no. There's always space for discoveries, (re)application, and innovation.

Or is this just my self-fulfilling expectation and illusion that what we know is everything there is to know 😮🤔🤔😅

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: there is always space mate and there is no way we know everything lol

author: @Hukkinen
text:
For a robot application, I'd like to have a small system that toggles a gear or a degree of freedom (in thumbl) when a rotating motor changes direction or is driven to the extreme end. I think it's possible 🤔

Mechanica and gears seem to be like programming (or language): you have a set of primitives which you then combine.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: me too!

author: @Hukkinen
text:
This really shows (or teaches me; not my field) that there are so many innovations yet to be made in this space 😮 Marvelous mechanism. 

However, compared to a bicycle, I feel we have a tradeoff in to an uneven force distribution in the parts. Some tiny surfaces get a lot of wear. But is this a problem?

How should we lubricate this? Can it be filled with oil (it wasn't a friction clutch, right?)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: no it is not a friction clutch so just grease it

author: @velcroman11
text: I don’t see how this device will work on the rear axle of a bicycle.  The device is mounted “inside” the spokes.  If I am understanding your mechanism properly, I believe you will need a pull lever in the centre of your device to change to drive ratio.  I refer you to the operation of a Sturmy Archer bicycle gear box.

author: @rose-ey6ct
text: Incredibly clever, but remember: Infinite variability also means infinite torque. For this reason, a "geared" neutral is a no no. As you move away from the geared neutral position, damage is inevitable. For a bicycle application, overall  losses will be too great.

replies.author: @glencahalin4786
replies.text: Though in a bicycle situation it won't have infinite resistance to constrain it, so only has to be strong enough to transmit enough power to cause wheel spin, which of course will vary with surface the tyre is on.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am not sure that's right mate - it doesn't mean infinite as never ending - The gears are a range between limits - the infinity comes in the steps between the gear range - but there is always a limit. Think of a number line going from zero to one. There is an infinite collection of numbers between the two but the range is always zero to one

author: @TheMadManPlace
text:
Brilliant Robert.
But don't think "bicycle", think small car.
It would have to be stronger which would increase the length a lot but not necessarily the diameter.
Because you no longer need the clutch and pressure plate, that space can also be used.
The only "problem" is what to do about reverse...
It is well known that internal combustion motors are most efficient at a constant RPM so the motor can run constantly at peak torque and this device could control the speed of the vehicle.

replies.author: @psychosomaticdragon
replies.text: i think a downside in vehicle use is the yoke mechanism - a bicycle isn't usually driven very fast at the crank, so with a decent bearing material on the interface it would last for a long time, but an engine would wear it down fast. It's why internal combustion engines still use cranks instead of yokes; they simply don't last long enough under those conditions.

replies.author: @gerryplayz4532
replies.text: I've seen some projects using a dedicated electric motor for reverse 😎👍

author: @snaawflake
text:
It's interesting that almost all CVTs seem to either rely on friction (V-Belt/Cone, NuVinci/epicycloidal, toroidal, Friction-disk, nutating disk, etc.) or on a ratcheting mechanism.
Hydrostatic/hydraulic CVTs/torque-converters seem to be the only exception (also discounting electrical motors hooked up to a generator here)

For bicycles it seems to me that the ratchet based ones make the most sense as you'd want a ratcheting mechanism in your bicycle anyways for freewheeling.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Do you know - I had the same thoughts mate - cheers

author: @charlesjmouse
text:
Always very interesting, educational, and potentially very useful.
I wonder... if there might be quite some losses or points of weakness going from 'cycloidal' to 'reciprocal', and then back to 'circular' movement? Does your 'Big Book of Gears' have anything to say on the subject of some preferably simpler and possibly more robust direct conversion from your cycloidal variator to circular output? Might that be built in to your planetary gears via one of your previous projects?
